---
title: "Week 2 — The OLS Engine"
subtitle: "ASSIP 8-Week Empirical Finance Camp"
author: "Prof. Lei Gao, George Mason University"
date: "2026-06-29"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    fig-width: 7.5
    fig-height: 4.2
    fig-dpi: 200
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
bibliography: ../bibliography.md
---

# Week 2 — The OLS Engine {.title}

::: notes
Welcome to Week 2. Last week we treated random variables as vectors and
learned what unbiasedness and consistency mean for a sample mean. This
week we cash that picture in for the workhorse of empirical finance:
ordinary least squares. By Friday you will derive OLS in matrix form,
state the five Gauss-Markov assumptions, partial variables out with
Frisch-Waugh-Lovell, fix your standard errors when the errors misbehave,
and recognize the three classic ways a regression can be biased. The
week runs Monday June 29 through Thursday July 2; July 3 is off. Keep
one phrase in your pocket all week: the point estimate is the easy part.
The standard error is the whole ballgame.
:::

## Learning Objectives

- Derive $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}$ by calculus and by geometry
- State the five Gauss-Markov assumptions; know which one is load-bearing
- Use Frisch-Waugh-Lovell to read any control coefficient as a partial slope
- Choose between classical, robust, clustered, and HAC standard errors
- Recognize omitted-variable bias, measurement error, and functional-form misspecification

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
OLS is one line of algebra and one geometric act: drop a perpendicular
onto the column space of the regressors.
:::

::: notes
Notice the verbs: derive, state, use, choose, recognize. None is
memorize. Each chapter follows the reveal-the-trick pattern. State the
result, see it on a tiny worked example, derive the algebra, then watch
where it fails. By Friday you should hand a stranger a regression
printout and tell them which standard-error column to trust.
:::

## Sam's Beta: OLS in Matrix Form

The model stacks one equation per observation into

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol\beta + \boldsymbol\varepsilon
$$

- $\mathbf{X}$ has a column of ones for the intercept, then regressors
- One derivation in matrix form covers every linear regression
- Sam's four-day toy: market $-2, 0, 1, 1$; stock $-1, 0, 3, 2$
- We will recover $\hat\beta_1 = 7/6 \approx 1.17$ by hand

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Matrix form means one derivation, done once, covers every linear
regression you will ever run.
:::

::: notes
Sam trades momentum names and wants to know how much his stock moves
when the market moves one percent. That number is beta, just a
regression slope. We start with four days small enough to invert
matrices by hand. Stack the outcomes into y, build X with a column of
ones and a column of market returns, and the whole regression becomes
y equals X beta plus epsilon. Matrix form is the gift that keeps
giving: when Sam adds ten controls next week, not a single symbol
changes.
:::

## The Normal Equations, Two Ways

Calculus: minimize $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})'(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})$, set gradient to zero.

Geometry: demand the residual be perpendicular to every regressor, $\mathbf{X}'\hat{\mathbf{e}} = \mathbf{0}$.

Both give:

$$
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y}
$$

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Minimizing squared residuals and dropping a perpendicular are the same
act. OLS is an orthogonality machine.
:::

::: notes
Here is the reveal. Two completely different routes land on the same
formula. Calculus takes the sum of squared residuals as a quadratic
bowl, takes the gradient, sets it to zero. Geometry takes no
derivatives. It says: if the residual still had any component along a
regressor, we could nudge the fit that way and shorten the residual.
So the residual must be perpendicular to every column of X. Write that
as X transpose e hat equals zero, plug in, and the normal equations
fall out.
:::

## Sam's Beta, By Hand

For Sam's four days,

$$
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{y} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 7/6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

- Residuals sum to zero exactly (intercept identity)
- Residuals orthogonal to market column: dot product equals zero
- Fitted values are the projection of $\mathbf{y}$ onto $\text{col}(\mathbf{X})$

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Beta of $7/6$ means Sam's stock amplifies the market by about 17%.
:::

::: notes
Watch what just happened. X transpose X is diagonal, four and six. X
transpose y is four and seven. Invert and multiply. Out pops beta naught
equals one and beta one equals seven sixths, about one point one seven.
Sam's stock amplifies the market by seventeen percent. Now check the
geometry: the four residuals sum to zero exactly, because we included
an intercept, and the residuals dotted with the market column is also
exactly zero. Not lucky — algebraic identities that least squares
mechanically forces.
:::

## Gauss-Markov: When OLS is BLUE

The five assumptions, as testable claims about Maya's loan data:

1. Linearity in parameters (not in $x$'s)
2. Random sampling + full column rank
3. **Zero conditional mean**: $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol\varepsilon \mid \mathbf{X}] = \mathbf{0}$
4. Homoskedasticity: $\text{Var}(\boldsymbol\varepsilon \mid \mathbf{X}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$
5. Normality of errors (only for finite-sample t-tests)

::: callout-warning
## The contract
"BLUE" is a contract with five clauses. Assumption 3 is the one that
matters most.
:::

::: notes
Maya regresses loan interest rates on borrower income and gets minus
zero point four two. Each extra ten thousand dollars of income, forty-
two basis points lower rate. Gauss-Markov says OLS is the Best Linear
Unbiased Estimator — but only if you sign all five clauses. Assumption
three, zero conditional mean, determines whether your coefficient means
anything causal. Weeks 3 and 4 exist because Assumption 3 is hard.
Chapter 2.4 will rip up Assumption 4. Assumption 5 is mostly irrelevant
in modern asymptotics.
:::

## Frisch-Waugh-Lovell: Reveal the Trick

A multiple-regression coefficient equals a simple-regression slope on
**residualized** variables.

Leah's R&D and patents, six firms, full multiple regression: $\hat\beta_1 = 1.838$.

1. Regress R&D on size, save residuals $\tilde x$
2. Regress patents on size, save residuals $\tilde y$
3. Slope of $\tilde y$ on $\tilde x$ is exactly $1.838$

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
"Controlling for $z$" means subtracting $z$'s linear footprint from
both sides. Within-group demeaning *is* fixed effects.
:::

::: notes
Leah studies innovation and regresses patents on R and D, controlling
for firm size. Her coefficient, one point eight three eight, is "the
effect of R and D, holding size fixed." Frisch-Waugh-Lovell says she
can get the same number a different way. Regress R and D on size, keep
the residual — the part of R and D that size cannot explain. Do the
same to patents. Regress the second residual on the first. Slope is
one point eight three eight, on the nose. Bonus: this is what fixed
effects do.
:::

## When Errors Misbehave: The Sandwich

OLS variance without homoskedasticity is a sandwich:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}\mid\mathbf{X})
= (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\boldsymbol\Omega\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}
$$

Four flavors of $\boldsymbol\Omega$:

- Classical: $\sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$ — assumes IID errors
- HC robust (White): unequal variances along the diagonal
- Cluster-robust: errors correlated within firm or lender
- HAC (Newey-West): errors correlated over time

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Same $\hat\beta$. Different $\boldsymbol\Omega$. Different t-stat.
Different conclusion.
:::

::: notes
The OLS point estimate is unbiased under Gauss-Markov regardless of
the error covariance. But the variance of beta hat is a sandwich:
outer slices of X transpose X inverse, filling of X transpose Omega X.
Omega is the error covariance matrix you never see — you estimate it.
Classical assumes Omega equals sigma squared times identity. In
finance, essentially always false. Each shape of Omega gets its own
estimator: White, cluster-robust, Newey-West HAC. Point estimate
never moves. Standard error can swing by a factor of three.
:::

## Maya's Megaphone Becomes a Whisper

Maya's fair-lending regression, $N = 9{,}000$ loans:

| Standard error type | SE   | t-stat |
|---------------------|------|--------|
| Classical           | 0.07 | 6.0    |
| HC robust           | 0.09 | 4.7    |
| Cluster by lender   | 0.19 | 2.2    |

- Same $\hat\beta_1 = 0.42$ in every row
- The $0.07$ was the lie; the $0.42$ was right the whole time
- See Petersen 2009 for the panel-data taxonomy

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
With clusters of size $n$, even mild within-cluster correlation can
multiply the true variance many times (Moulton 1986).
:::

::: notes
Same Maya. Same nine thousand loans. Same coefficient of zero point
four two. Three different standard errors, three different t-stats,
three different career outcomes. Classical says screaming six-sigma.
White's robust knocks it to four point seven. Clustering by lender —
the right thing to do, because lenders set rates in correlated ways —
drops the t-stat to two point two. The point estimate did not move a
hair. Petersen 2009 is required reading for panel regressions.
:::

## Three Ways the Coefficient is Wrong

Beyond standard errors, the *point estimate itself* can be biased:

- **Omitted variable bias**: bias $= \beta_2 \cdot \delta_1$
- **Measurement error in $x$**: attenuation toward zero
- **Functional-form misspecification**: forcing a line through a curve

::: callout-warning
## Master fault
Each is a different way $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon \mid x] = 0$ breaks.
A precise standard error on a biased coefficient is precisely wrong.
:::

::: notes
Standard errors say nothing about whether the number you wobble around
is the right number. Three classic ways your coefficient points wrong.
Omitted variable bias: leave out a relevant variable correlated with
your regressor, your coefficient absorbs its effect. Bias equals beta
two times delta one — the omitted variable's effect times its slope on
the included regressor. Measurement error pulls slopes toward zero,
called attenuation. Functional form dumps missing curvature into
epsilon. All three are failures of zero conditional mean. This chapter
is the bridge to Weeks 3 and 4.
:::

## Common Pitfalls

1. Reporting classical t-stats on clustered data
2. Dummy variable trap: every category plus an intercept
3. Reading a control coefficient as causal (the table 2 fallacy)
4. Trusting in-sample $R^2$ as out-of-sample evidence

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
Each of these has been published and retracted.
:::

::: notes
The classical-SE-on-clustered-data mistake is the single most common
referee complaint in finance. The only coefficient you can interpret
causally is the one whose exogeneity you actually defended, not the
controls hanging off it. Bring the discipline: write the one-line spec,
defend the standard-error choice, and only causally interpret the
coefficient your identification strategy was designed to recover.
:::

## Deliverable + Look-Ahead

- **Lab 2**: Fama-MacBeth on CRSP (or seeded synthetic fallback)
- **Mentor session**: "Why your standard error is the whole ballgame"
- **Assessment**: derive OLS, interpret an FWL printout, choose an SE

Look ahead to Week 3:

- Zero conditional mean finally fails on purpose
- Causal inference, potential outcomes, the credibility revolution

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
You have the engine. Week 3 is about when the engine measures cause,
not correlation.
:::

::: notes
Thursday's lab is Fama and MacBeth 1973, the most famous two-pass
procedure in asset pricing. Estimate a beta per stock, run cross-
sectional regressions month by month, average the slopes. The scatter
of those slopes divided by root T is your standard error, automatically
handling within-month cross-sectional correlation. We plant a known
premium in synthetic data so you can check your machine. Next week we
finally drop the pretense that Assumption 3 holds by accident.
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

::: notes
References auto-populated by Pandoc from cited keys including Fama and
MacBeth 1973, Petersen 2009, Moulton 1986, and Gao et al. 2024.
:::
